Chapter 4 — The real cause: representation¶

Chapter 3 ruled out every fixable training cause — settling, curriculum, capacity, coverage, reward variance, the standard knobs. What survives is a one-directional over-doubling bias in a handful of boundary cells. By now the oscillation is cured and training is long (5M episodes), so the residual is neither variance nor under-training; a bias in specific cells is a statement about how the network represents the map.

The finding, in one line: the smooth network learns everything the problem needs — the ordinal structure, the whole hit/stand map — except a sharp wall at the double's edge. That one missing wall is the entire residual, and the input encoding is a dial for how much of it you can buy back. We show it three ways: where the errors sit (§4.1), the geometry that puts them there (§4.2), and the dial that moves them (§4.3).

4.1 The residual is settled — a soft edge on the double¶

Take a long, stacked run (scalar, flat-then-decay, 5M). The wild double of Chapter 2 is gone — Q(double) on soft-20 v8 has settled (back-half std 0.16, down from the naive 1.14). What remains is not noise. The grid below is double-focused: correct doubles green, over-doubles red, under-doubles orange, everything else gray (cells carry the agent's action, agent→basic where they differ). The red cells are exactly the ones bordering the correct-double region — the double's edge, off by one cell. On this smooth (scalar) run every error is an over-double: the double region bleeds outward because its boundary is a ramp, not a wall. That soft edge — settled, one-directional, sitting right on the boundary — is the whole story of the chapter; under-doubles (orange) only appear once the encoding is sharpened (§4.3).

In [1]:
import sys; sys.path.insert(0, '.')
import json as _j, statistics as st
from pathlib import Path as _P
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import ListedColormap
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from runs import load_runs, learning_curve, show, provenance, fignote, describe

df = load_runs(); dqn = df[df.has_model & (df.method == 'dqn')]
RUN = dqn[(dqn.encoding == 'scalar') & (dqn.lr_hold_until > 0) & (dqn.episodes == 5_000_000)].iloc[0]
cells = _j.load(open(_P(RUN.path) / 'record.json', encoding='utf-8'))['diff']['cells']

UP = list(range(2, 12))
ACT = {'hit': 'H', 'stand': 'S', 'double': 'D'}
DCOL = ['#bfe6b6', '#e24b4a', '#f4a259', '#e7e7e7']   # 0 correct double, 1 over, 2 under, 3 other
dcmap = ListedColormap(DCOL)
def dcat(c):
    a, b = c['agent_action'], c['basic_action']
    if b == 'double' and a == 'double': return 0
    if b != 'double' and a == 'double': return 1   # over-double
    if b == 'double' and a != 'double': return 2   # under-double
    return 3
def dpanel(ax, clist, is_soft, title):
    vals = sorted({c['player_value'] for c in clist if c['is_soft'] == is_soft}, reverse=True)
    G = np.full((len(vals), len(UP)), np.nan)
    for c in clist:
        if c['is_soft'] != is_soft: continue
        i, j = vals.index(c['player_value']), UP.index(c['dealer_upcard'])
        G[i, j] = dcat(c)
        lab = ACT.get(c['agent_action'], '?')
        if c['agent_action'] != c['basic_action']:
            lab = '%s→%s' % (ACT.get(c['agent_action'], '?'), ACT.get(c['basic_action'], '?'))
        ax.text(j, i, lab, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=6.5)
    ax.imshow(G, cmap=dcmap, vmin=0, vmax=3, aspect='auto')
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(UP))); ax.set_xticklabels([str(u) if u < 11 else 'A' for u in UP], fontsize=8)
    ax.set_yticks(range(len(vals))); ax.set_yticklabels(vals, fontsize=7)
    ax.set_xlabel('dealer upcard', fontsize=9); ax.set_ylabel('player total', fontsize=9); ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
def dgrid(clist, run_row, suptitle):
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 6))
    dpanel(axes[0], clist, False, 'Hard totals'); dpanel(axes[1], clist, True, 'Soft totals')
    handles = [mpatches.Patch(color=DCOL[0], label='correct double'), mpatches.Patch(color=DCOL[1], label='over-double'),
               mpatches.Patch(color=DCOL[2], label='under-double'), mpatches.Patch(color=DCOL[3], label='other')]
    fig.legend(handles=handles, loc='upper center', ncol=4, fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9, bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, 0.97))
    fig.suptitle(suptitle, y=1.0, fontsize=11)
    plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.92]); fignote(provenance(run_row, role='%s 5M flat-then-decay' % run_row.encoding)); plt.show()

dgrid(cells, RUN, "Scalar (smooth): the over-doubles (red) ring the correct doubles (green), off by one cell")
dd = [cp['probe_q']['soft20_v8']['double'] for cp in learning_curve(RUN.path) if cp.get('probe_q') and 'soft20_v8' in cp.get('probe_q', {})]
gen = [c for c in cells if c['category'] == 'genuine_disagreement']
over = sum(1 for c in gen if c['agent_action'] == 'double' and c['basic_action'] != 'double')
print('Q(double) soft-20 v8 back-half std = %.2f  (Ch2 naive was 1.14 — now settled)  |  %d of %d genuine disagreements are over-doubles'
      % (st.pstdev(dd[len(dd)//2:]), over, len(gen)))

Q(double) soft-20 v8 back-half std = 0.16  (Ch2 naive was 1.14 — now settled)  |  21 of 23 genuine disagreements are over-doubles

4.2 Two geometries — the smooth net has everything but the wall¶

Why that soft edge? Read the network's own geometry, for both encodings. Reload the weights, take the penultimate-layer activation for every cell (the features the Q-head sees), and compare scalar (total and upcard as single ordered numbers — smooth, ordinal) against one-hot (each as an unordered categorical block — no axis to ramp along). What you should see: scalar lays the ordered inputs (soft/hard, upcard) out as clean structure and carves coherent hit / stand / double regions — it has everything — and the one soft spot is the double's boundary, where the errors sit. The silhouettes put a number on the structure; the margin shows confidence collapsing on that seam.

A note on projection. The action view uses PCA — the hit→double→stand decision is a global ordering that PCA's linear axes preserve and t-SNE tends to fragment into islands. The other four views (category, margin, soft/hard, upcard) use t-SNE, which separated the local clusters and the error seam more clearly here. Different tools for global structure vs local clusters — not a projection picked to flatter each claim.

In [2]:
ROOT = next(p for p in [_P.cwd(), *_P.cwd().parents] if (p / 'blackjack_rl').is_dir())
if str(ROOT) not in sys.path: sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT))
from blackjack_rl.evaluation.embedding import load_agent, cell_embeddings
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

A = dqn[(dqn.encoding == 'onehot') & (dqn.lr_hold_until > 0) & (dqn.episodes == 5_000_000)].iloc[0]  # sharp / categorical
B = RUN                                                                                              # smooth / ordinal (scalar, from 4.1)
def embed(row):
    ce = cell_embeddings(load_agent(row.path)); X = np.array(ce.embeddings)
    return dict(label='%s (%s)' % (row.encoding, row.run[:13]), cells=ce.cells, X=X,
                pca=PCA(n_components=2).fit_transform(X),
                tsne=TSNE(n_components=2, perplexity=30, init='pca', random_state=0).fit_transform(X))
EA, EB = embed(A), embed(B)

ACOL = {'hit': '#378ADD', 'stand': '#1D9E75', 'double': '#D85A30'}
CAT  = {'agree': '#CBCBC4', 'near_equal_ev': '#EF9F27', 'genuine_disagreement': '#E24B4A'}
SOFTC = {'soft': '#7F77DD', 'hard': '#9AA0A6'}
PAL  = {'action': ACOL, 'category': CAT, 'soft': SOFTC}
KEY  = {'action': lambda c: c['action'], 'category': lambda c: c['category'], 'soft': lambda c: 'soft' if c['is_soft'] else 'hard'}
CONT = {'margin': ('q_margin', 'viridis'), 'upcard': ('dealer_upcard', 'coolwarm')}
def scat(ax, P, labels, colors, title):
    for lab in dict.fromkeys(labels):
        m = [i for i, l in enumerate(labels) if l == lab]
        ax.scatter(P[m, 0], P[m, 1], s=20, c=colors.get(lab, '#888'), label=str(lab), edgecolor='none', alpha=0.85)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([]); ax.legend(fontsize=7, framealpha=0.9)
def compare(kind, proj):
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
    for j, E in enumerate([EA, EB]):
        P = E[proj]
        if kind in CONT:
            field, cmap = CONT[kind]
            sc = ax[j].scatter(P[:, 0], P[:, 1], s=20, c=[c[field] for c in E['cells']], cmap=cmap)
            ax[j].set_title('%s — %s' % (E['label'], kind), fontsize=10); ax[j].set_xticks([]); ax[j].set_yticks([])
            fig.colorbar(sc, ax=ax[j], fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
        else:
            scat(ax[j], P, [KEY[kind](c) for c in E['cells']], PAL[kind], '%s — %s' % (E['label'], kind))
    fig.suptitle('%s . %s    (left: one-hot / sharp   |   right: scalar / smooth)' % (proj.upper(), kind), y=1.03)
    plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

compare('action', 'pca')                       # global hit->double->stand ordering: PCA keeps it, t-SNE fragments it
for k in ['category', 'margin', 'soft', 'upcard']:
    compare(k, 'tsne')                         # local clusters + the error seam: t-SNE separates them more clearly

def sil(E, key):
    labs = [key(c) for c in E['cells']]; code = {a: i for i, a in enumerate(dict.fromkeys(labs))}
    return silhouette_score(E['X'], [code[l] for l in labs])
print('ORDINAL STRUCTURE (silhouette on the full embedding; higher = the ordered feature is laid out as clean structure):')
print('  %-22s%12s%10s' % ('', 'soft/hard', 'upcard'))
for E in [EA, EB]:
    print('  %-22s%+12.3f%+10.3f' % (E['label'][:22], sil(E, lambda c: c['is_soft']), sil(E, lambda c: c['dealer_upcard'])))
m_a = [c['q_margin'] for c in EB['cells'] if c['category'] == 'agree']
m_d = [c['q_margin'] for c in EB['cells'] if c['category'] == 'genuine_disagreement']
print('\nERRORS ON THE SEAM (scalar): mean margin  agree %.2f  vs  disagree %.2f (razor-edge).' % (np.mean(m_a), np.mean(m_d)))
print('scalar leads the structure rows (smooth ordinal axes); one-hot scrambles them but draws the sharper double boundary.')

ORDINAL STRUCTURE (silhouette on the full embedding; higher = the ordered feature is laid out as clean structure):
                           soft/hard    upcard
  onehot (20260622-0158)      +0.154    -0.064
  scalar (20260621-2049)      +0.112    -0.030

ERRORS ON THE SEAM (scalar): mean margin  agree 0.46  vs  disagree 0.28 (razor-edge).
scalar leads the structure rows (smooth ordinal axes); one-hot scrambles them but draws the sharper double boundary.

4.3 The dial — how much wall you can buy¶

If the residual is one missing wall, a sharper input should build some of it back. One-hot treats each total / upcard as its own slot, so it can place a hard edge between adjacent cells instead of ramping across them. Same network, schedule, and 5M budget — only the encoding differs. The same double-focused grid, now for one-hot, makes it visual; the table puts numbers on it.

In [3]:
# the SAME double-focused grid (reusing dgrid from 4.1), now for ONE-HOT — compare to the scalar grid above
A = dqn[(dqn.encoding == 'onehot') & (dqn.lr_hold_until > 0) & (dqn.episodes == 5_000_000)].iloc[0]
oh_cells = _j.load(open(_P(A.path) / 'record.json', encoding='utf-8'))['diff']['cells']
dgrid(oh_cells, A, "One-hot (sharp): the over-double ring THINS (21→9 cells) and a few under-doubles (orange) appear")
In [4]:
def breakdown(path):
    dc = _j.load(open(_P(path) / 'record.json', encoding='utf-8'))['diff']['cells']
    dis = [c for c in dc if c['category'] == 'genuine_disagreement']
    over = sum(1 for c in dis if c['agent_action'] == 'double' and c['basic_action'] != 'double')
    under = sum(1 for c in dis if c['basic_action'] == 'double' and c['agent_action'] != 'double')
    return len(dis), over, under

import pandas as pd
rows = []
for tag, R in [('one-hot (sharp)', A), ('scalar (smooth)', RUN)]:
    g, o, u = breakdown(R.path)
    rows.append({'encoding': tag, 'agreement': R.agreement, 'edge': R.edge_pct, 'over-doubles': o, 'under-doubles': u})
show(pd.DataFrame(rows), pct=['agreement'], num=['edge'],
     caption='Turn the dial to SHARP (one-hot) and the over-doubles more than halve (21 → 9) — the direct test of the '
             'smoothness diagnosis. Here the residual IS a boundary problem, so one-hot wins both agreement and edge; '
             'the price is a couple of under-doubles (no neighbour to interpolate a double in from).',
     source='source: matched 5M flat-then-decay (onehot vs scalar), [64,64] · stand · double@500k · Double-DQN · seed 42')

def agreement_curve(path):
    return [(cp['episode'], cp['agreement']) for cp in learning_curve(path) if cp.get('agreement') is not None]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.5, 4.5))
for tag, R, col in [('one-hot (sharp boundary)', A, '#D85A30'), ('scalar (ordinal/smooth)', RUN, '#378ADD')]:
    c = agreement_curve(R.path)
    ax.plot([e for e, _ in c], [a for _, a in c], color=col, lw=1.4, label=tag)
ax.axvline(500_000, ls='--', color='#999', lw=1); ax.text(515_000, 0.70, 'double introduced', fontsize=8, color='#666')
ax.set_xlabel('training episode'); ax.set_ylabel('agreement'); ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc='lower right')
ax.set_title('The crossover: scalar leads the hit/stand phase, one-hot overtakes once the double enters')
fignote('source: two single runs · 5M flat-then-decay · seed 42 (onehot / scalar)'); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
Turn the dial to SHARP (one-hot) and the over-doubles more than halve (21 → 9) — the direct test of the smoothness diagnosis. Here the residual IS a boundary problem, so one-hot wins both agreement and edge; the price is a couple of under-doubles (no neighbour to interpolate a double in from).
encoding agreement edge over-doubles under-doubles
one-hot (sharp) 92.9% 0.90 9 3
scalar (smooth) 87.5% 1.25 21 0
source: matched 5M flat-then-decay (onehot vs scalar), [64,64] · stand · double@500k · Double-DQN · seed 42

The crossover is the proof that the double is the only place this bites. While just the hit/stand decision exists, the smooth net is fine — it even leads, on ordinal generalization. The instant the double is introduced, one-hot overtakes and stays ahead. Hit/stand has comfortable margins and a monotone-ish structure smoothness handles; the double is a thin, hard-edged window — the one wall the smooth net can't draw. The encoding is a single knob between two regimes:

  • scalar = smooth / ordinal — clean ordered axes and interpolation across neighbours: strong structure and rare-cell generalization, but a soft double boundary, so it over-doubles at the seam.
  • one-hot = categorical / sharp — hard walls between adjacent cells: half the over-doubles, at the cost of the ordinal structure (a few under-doubles, weaker generalization).

Which end wins is decided by what the residual is. For Problem A it is the double's soft edge, so sharp wins. The point is not "one-hot is better" — it is that the error was one missing wall, and the dial controls how much of it you build.

4.4 Conclusion — the floor is one wall¶

The residual is not instability (cured, Ch2–3), not under-training (5M), not coverage or reward variance (Ch3). It is the one boundary a smooth approximator cannot make sharp: the double's edge. A continuous representation places adjacent cells side by side and interpolates, so a step that should be a wall becomes a ramp, and the cells on the ramp fall to the wrong side — the over-doubles. The embedding shows them on the low-margin seam; the encoding dial confirms it — sharpen the input and half the wall returns.

This closes the loop with Chapter 1. A lookup table is all wall: every cell independent, every boundary hard, zero tuning — which is exactly why it wins on agreement-exactness. The network's gap is the softness of that one edge. But on edge — the metric that costs money — a sharp-enough (one-hot) net draws it clean enough to match or beat the table. So the gap that remains is a representation choice (how much wall you build), not a limit on what the network can learn. Chapter 7 returns here as the project's spine — the three axes of Chapter 1's decision-tree frame, now earned.
